feynman.0: Forces are what change motion — but here’s a lovely shortcut: two quantities, momentum and energy, refuse to change when nothing from outside meddles, and that lets you size up a whole collision or launch without ever knowing the force at each instant. On this page we’ll read impulse straight off the area under a force–time graph, sort collisions into elastic or inelastic by keeping our eye on the kinetic energy, and follow energy as it slides between kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms and finally leaks away into the surroundings.
feynman.1: The momentum of an object is just its mass times its velocity, p = mv. Now, velocity is a vector, so momentum is a vector too — it points the very same way the velocity does. In one dimension we handle all of that with a simple bookkeeping trick: pick a positive direction and call motion the other way negative.
feynman.2: A net force changes an object’s momentum. Newton’s second law, in its fullest form, says the net force is the rate of change of momentum — so over a stretch of time Δt the net force builds up a change in momentum we call the impulse:
feynman.3: Impulse and change in momentum are really one and the same thing, just measured two different ways, which is why 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹. The change in velocity is the final minus the initial, Δv = v − u, and you’ve got to keep track of signs: a ball that bounces back reverses direction, so its velocity flips sign and the impulse can come out bigger than what you’d need merely to stop it dead.
feynman.4: When the force wobbles around during the interval, the F in FΔt means the average force. But here’s the nice part: you can read the impulse straight off a force–time graph as the area between the graph and the time axis. It works no matter what shape the graph takes — a rectangle for a steady force, a triangle for one that rises and falls in straight lines, or a trapezium for a force that ramps up, holds, and ramps back down.
feynman.5: Trap Momentum is a vector — don’t forget it. A 2.0 kg trolley going at 3.0 m s⁻¹ that rebounds at 3.0 m s⁻¹ has not had zero change in momentum, even though the speeds match. Call the original direction positive: the momentum swings from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always pin + and − onto opposite directions, and stick with that choice the whole way through a problem.
feynman.6: Mei gives a stationary 1.5 kg trolley a push along a frictionless track. A force sensor records what she applies as the trapezoidal pulse in Figure 4.1: the force climbs in a straight line from 0 to 40 N over the first 0.10 s, sits at 40 N until 0.30 s, then drops in a straight line back to 0 at 0.40 s. The trolley is at rest before she pushes.
feynman.7: Formula: FΔt = mΔv, where the impulse FΔt is just the area under the force–time graph.
feynman.9: Answer: the impulse is 12 N s, so Δv = FΔt / m = 12 ÷ 1.5 = 8.0 m s⁻¹. The trolley started from rest, so its final velocity is 8.0 m s⁻¹ in the direction she pushed.
feynman.10: An isolated system is just one that feels no net external force. The total momentum of such a system never changes: whatever momentum one object picks up, another gives up by exactly the same amount — and the reason is beautifully simple, because the forces the objects push on each other with are a Newton’s third law pair, acting for the very same time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and it holds in every collision and explosion you can dream up, whether the objects deform, stick, bounce or shatter to bits.
feynman.11: In one dimension, once you’ve picked a positive direction, conservation of momentum is written:
feynman.12: Kinetic energy is a different story altogether. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy afterward exactly matches the total before. In an inelastic one the total afterward comes out smaller; but that missing energy isn’t destroyed — it’s just turned into other forms: heat, sound, and the energy of bending things permanently out of shape. A collision where the objects stick together is perfectly inelastic, and it sheds the most kinetic energy you can possibly lose while still conserving momentum.
feynman.13: To pin down which kind of collision you’ve got, you use momentum conservation first to dig out the unknown velocities, then you work out the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² before and after. If the two totals come out equal, the collision is elastic; if the after-total is smaller, it’s inelastic, and that shortfall is exactly the energy that got dumped into the surroundings.
feynman.14: Key idea Momentum is conserved in every collision in an isolated system. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. If Ek after is less than Ek before, the collision is inelastic and the missing energy has gone off as heat, sound and deformation.
feynman.15: Trap “Energy is lost” never means energy just vanishes — it can’t. Total energy is always conserved, full stop. In an inelastic collision it’s the kinetic energy that drops; and every last joule of it is accounted for, showing up as heat, sound and the work spent permanently deforming the things that collided.
feynman.16: On a frictionless air track, cart A of mass 0.80 kg rolls at 3.0 m s⁻¹ to the right and runs into a stationary cart B of mass 0.40 kg. The carts couple up and move off together as one. Sora takes the right as positive and treats the two carts as an isolated system.
feynman.18: Substitution: (0.80 × 3.0) + (0.40 × 0) = (0.80 + 0.40) × v, so v = 2.4 ÷ 1.2 = 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right.
feynman.19: Formula (classify): compare Ek = ½mv² before and after.
feynman.20: Substitution: Ek before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J; Ek after = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J.
feynman.21: Answer: the kinetic energy drops from 3.6 J to 2.4 J, so the collision is inelastic — perfectly inelastic, in fact, since the carts stuck. The 1.2 J that went missing got dumped into the surroundings as heat, sound and deformation. Momentum, though, doesn’t budge: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.
feynman.22: Work is the energy handed to an object by a force pushing it through a displacement. For a steady force acting along the line of motion, the work done is simply the force times the displacement:
feynman.23: When the force changes as the object moves, the work done is the area under a force–distance graph. It’s the very same area trick we used for impulse on a force–time graph, and the one we’ll use for elastic energy on a force–extension graph: every time, the area of a force-versus-something graph hands you the total built-up effect of the force.
feynman.24: Only the part of the force along the displacement does any work. A force pointing perpendicular to the displacement does zero work, because it has no piece pointing the way the object is moving. And that’s exactly why the speed in uniform circular motion stays put: the net force aims at the centre, the velocity runs tangent to the circle, the two sit at right angles — so the centripetal force pours no energy into the object at all. It keeps swinging the object’s direction around without ever touching its speed.
feynman.25: The energy an object has simply because it’s moving is its kinetic energy:
feynman.26: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy — a net force doing positive work speeds it up, one doing negative work slows it down. And because kinetic energy goes as the square of the speed, double the speed and you quadruple the kinetic energy.
feynman.27: Key idea A force perpendicular to the displacement does zero work. The centripetal force in uniform circular motion is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work and the speed stays constant even while the velocity keeps changing direction.
feynman.28: Connects This is why a satellite in a circular orbit, and a car cruising at steady speed around a circular road, both hold a constant speed: gravity and friction respectively pull toward the centre, square to the motion, and transfer no energy. And that work-as-area rule pops up again for springs in the next section.
feynman.29: An ideal spring obeys Hooke’s law: the force it pushes back with is proportional to how far you’ve stretched or squashed it. Writing just the magnitudes, the applied force you need to hold the spring at extension x is:
feynman.30: The spring constant k is the gradient of the force–extension graph — a stiffer spring gives a steeper line. So the graph of applied force against extension is a straight line right through the origin, with gradient k.
feynman.31: The elastic potential energy stashed in a stretched or squashed spring is just the work you did deforming it, which is the area under the force–extension graph. That graph is a triangle of base x and height kx, so the area is ½ × x × kx:
feynman.32: The stored energy hangs on the square of the extension. Double the extension and you double the force but quadruple the stored energy — because the triangle’s area grows along with both its base and its height at once.
feynman.33: Trap Es = ½kx² is quadratic in x; it is not kx and it is not ½kx. The factor of ½ comes from the triangular area under the force–extension graph, and the square means a spring stretched twice as far stores four times the energy, not twice. Don’t mix up the force F = kx (the height of the graph) with the energy Es = ½kx² (the area sitting under it).
feynman.34: A spring launcher has a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego squashes it down by 0.080 m, sets a 0.20 kg glider against it on a frictionless horizontal track, and lets go. Every bit of the stored elastic energy passes straight into the glider as kinetic energy.
feynman.35: Formula: Es = ½kx², then energy conservation Es = Ek = ½mv².
feynman.36: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J. Then ½ × 0.20 × v² = 0.80, so v² = 8.0.
feynman.37: Answer: v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹, headed along the track the way the spring pushes.
feynman.38: Energy is never made and never destroyed; it only gets turned from one form into another. The three mechanical forms in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy, the energy you bank by lifting a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
feynman.39: This expression only holds in a uniform field — near Earth’s surface, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ is as good as constant over the heights we deal with. For the far bigger height changes of a satellite, g shifts, and you’ve got to get Eg instead from the area under a force–distance graph, or from the area under a field–distance graph times the mass (we tackle that in the Fields chapter).
feynman.40: In an isolated system with nothing dissipative going on, the total mechanical energy — the kinetic, elastic and gravitational potential energies all added up — stays constant. A falling ball trades gravitational potential energy for kinetic energy; a launched spring turns elastic potential energy first into kinetic energy and then, as the thing climbs, into gravitational potential energy. Following that total is often quicker than wrestling with forces and kinematics, because the route from start to finish simply doesn’t matter.
feynman.41: A mass dangling on a spring and set bobbing is one continuous swapping of energy back and forth. At the lowest point the spring is stretched most, so elastic potential energy is at its peak and kinetic energy is zero; as the mass climbs the spring eases off and the mass speeds up, so the elastic and gravitational stores pour into kinetic energy; at the highest point the kinetic energy is back to zero again. In an ideal setup the total holds steady and the bobbing goes on forever; in a real one a little energy slips away each cycle and the motion gradually dies out.
feynman.42: Real systems are never perfectly isolated. Friction, drag and the crumpling of colliding bodies dissipate mechanical energy into the surroundings as heat, sound and permanent deformation. When that happens the mechanical energy goes down, but the total energy — now counting in the heat and sound made along the way — is still conserved. The energy books only balance exactly once you’ve put those dissipated forms back in.
feynman.43: Key idea In an isolated system with no dissipation, total mechanical energy (kinetic + elastic + gravitational) is conserved. When friction, drag or deformation act, mechanical energy is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is still conserved once those forms are counted.
feynman.44: The launcher from the previous example is now stood up vertically. The same spring (k = 250 N m⁻¹) is squashed by 0.080 m and a 0.20 kg ball is set on top. The ball is released and rises freely. Priya wants the highest the ball gets above its release point, ignoring air resistance, and takes g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
feynman.45: Formula: with no dissipation, the stored elastic potential energy becomes gravitational potential energy at the top, where the ball is momentarily at rest: Es = mgΔh.
feynman.46: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J, so 0.80 = 0.20 × 9.8 × Δh.
feynman.47: Answer: Δh = 0.80 ÷ (0.20 × 9.8) = 0.41 m above the release point. Air resistance, which bleeds energy off as heat and sound, would knock this height down.
feynman.48: Connects The very same energy bookkeeping runs a projectile: a ball thrown upward trades kinetic energy for gravitational potential energy on the way up and trades it right back on the way down, hitting the same speed at any given height (with no air resistance around). The whole flight, the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is g downward even while the ball is still climbing. Air resistance bleeds energy away and pulls down both the maximum height and the range.

sagan.0: Forces are forever pushing and pulling at the motion of things, yet hidden beneath all that turmoil lie two quantities that nature refuses to let go of — momentum and energy — and by following them we can understand an entire collision or launch without ever needing to know the force at each fleeting instant. This page reads impulse as the area beneath a force–time graph, sorts collisions into elastic and inelastic by tracing what becomes of the kinetic energy, and tracks energy itself as it flows among kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms and slips away, at last, into the wider world.
sagan.1: The momentum of an object is simply its mass woven together with its velocity, p = mv. And because velocity is a vector, momentum too must be a vector: it points the same way the motion does. In one dimension we tame this by choosing a positive direction, and letting whatever moves the other way carry a negative sign.
sagan.2: A net force changes an object's momentum. Newton's second law, in its fullest and most general form, declares that the net force equals the rate at which momentum changes; so across a stretch of time Δt the net force builds up a change in momentum we call the impulse:
sagan.3: Impulse and change in momentum are one and the same thing seen through two different lenses, so 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹. The change in velocity is the final velocity less the initial, Δv = v − u, and we must follow the signs faithfully: a ball that rebounds turns its motion clean around, so its velocity changes sign, and the impulse can be larger than the one needed merely to bring it to rest.
sagan.4: When the force shifts and wavers through the interval, the F in FΔt is the average force. The impulse can be read straight off a force–time graph as the area between the graph and the time axis. And this holds true whatever the shape the graph may take: a rectangle for a constant force, a triangle for a force that rises and falls in a straight line, or a trapezium for a force that climbs, holds steady, and eases back down.
sagan.5: Trap Momentum is a vector. A 2.0 kg trolley travelling at 3.0 m s⁻¹ that rebounds at 3.0 m s⁻¹ has not somehow suffered zero change in momentum. Taking the original direction as positive, the momentum swings from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always grant + and − to opposite directions, and hold to that choice steadfastly throughout a problem.
sagan.6: Mei pushes a stationary 1.5 kg trolley along a frictionless track. A force sensor records the force she applies as the trapezoidal pulse of Figure 4.1: the force rises linearly from 0 to 40 N over the first 0.10 s, holds at 40 N until 0.30 s, then falls linearly to 0 at 0.40 s. The trolley is at rest before the push.
sagan.7: Formula: FΔt = mΔv, with the impulse FΔt equal to the area under the force–time graph.
sagan.9: Answer: the impulse is 12 N s, so Δv = FΔt / m = 12 ÷ 1.5 = 8.0 m s⁻¹. The trolley started at rest, so its final velocity is 8.0 m s⁻¹ in the direction of the push.
sagan.10: An isolated system is one upon which no net external force lays a hand. The total momentum of such a system never changes: whatever momentum one object gains, another surrenders in precisely the same measure, because the forces the objects press upon each other form a Newton's third law pair, acting for one and the same span of time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and it holds true in every collision and explosion the universe has ever staged — whether the objects deform, stick, bounce or shatter into pieces.
sagan.11: In one dimension, with a positive direction chosen, conservation of momentum is written:
sagan.12: Kinetic energy is another story altogether. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy afterward matches the total before, to the last joule. In an inelastic collision the total kinetic energy after falls short of the total before; yet that difference is not destroyed — nothing in nature is ever truly destroyed — but transformed into other forms: heat, sound, and the energy locked into permanent deformation. A collision in which the objects cling together is perfectly inelastic, and it sheds the greatest kinetic energy that the conservation of momentum will allow.
sagan.13: To classify a collision, we lean first on momentum conservation to uncover the unknown velocities, then we reckon the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² both before and after. If the two totals stand equal, the collision is elastic; if the total after has shrunk, the collision is inelastic, and that shortfall is the energy spilled out into the environment.
sagan.14: Key idea Momentum is conserved in every collision in an isolated system. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. If Ek after is less than Ek before, the collision is inelastic and the missing energy has become heat, sound and deformation.
sagan.15: Trap "Energy is lost" never means energy vanishes from the cosmos. Total energy is always conserved. In an inelastic collision it is the kinetic energy that dwindles; and that energy is accounted for, every last bit of it, as heat, sound, and the work done permanently deforming the colliding objects.
sagan.16: On a frictionless air track, cart A of mass 0.80 kg moves at 3.0 m s⁻¹ to the right and collides with a stationary cart B of mass 0.40 kg. The carts couple together and move off as one. Sora takes the right as positive and treats the two carts as an isolated system.
sagan.18: Substitution: (0.80 × 3.0) + (0.40 × 0) = (0.80 + 0.40) × v, so v = 2.4 ÷ 1.2 = 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right.
sagan.19: Formula (classify): compare Ek = ½mv² before and after.
sagan.20: Substitution: Ek before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J; Ek after = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J.
sagan.21: Answer: the kinetic energy falls from 3.6 J to 2.4 J, so the collision is inelastic (in fact perfectly inelastic, because the carts stick). The 1.2 J difference is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation. Momentum, however, is conserved: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.
sagan.22: Work is the energy handed over to an object by a force acting through a displacement. For a constant force pressing along the line of motion, the work done is simply the force multiplied by the displacement:
sagan.23: When the force changes from place to place, the work done becomes the area under a force–distance graph. This area rule is the very same one we met for impulse on a force–time graph, and for elastic energy on a force–extension graph: in each case the area of a force-versus-something graph gathers up the accumulated effect of the force.
sagan.24: Only the part of the force lying along the displacement does any work. A force aimed perpendicular to the displacement does zero work, for it has no foothold in the direction of motion. This is the very reason the speed in uniform circular motion holds constant: the net force points unwaveringly to the centre, the velocity sweeps out tangentially, and the two stand at right angles — so the centripetal force pours no energy into the object at all. It forever bends the object's direction without ever quickening or slowing its pace.
sagan.25: The energy an object holds simply by virtue of its motion is its kinetic energy:
sagan.26: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: a net force that does positive work hurries the object along, and one that does negative work reins it in. And because kinetic energy hangs on the square of the speed, doubling the speed quadruples the kinetic energy.
sagan.27: Key idea A force perpendicular to the displacement does zero work. The centripetal force in uniform circular motion is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work and the speed stays constant even though the velocity changes direction.
sagan.28: Connects This is why a satellite wheeling in a circular orbit, and a car holding steady speed on a circular road, keep one unchanging speed: gravity and friction respectively pull toward the centre, perpendicular to the motion, and transfer no energy. The work-as-area rule will rise again for springs in the next section.

sagan.30: The spring constant k is the gradient of the force–extension graph: a stiffer spring carves a steeper line. The graph of applied force against extension is therefore a straight line marching out from the origin, of gradient k.
sagan.31: The elastic potential energy stored within a stretched or compressed spring is the work done in deforming it, which is the area under the force–extension graph. Because that graph is a triangle of base x and height kx, the area is ½ × x × kx:
sagan.32: The stored energy hangs upon the square of the extension. Doubling the extension doubles the force but quadruples the stored energy, because the triangle's area swells with both its base and its height together.
sagan.33: Trap Es = ½kx² is quadratic in x; it is not kx and not ½kx. The factor of ½ springs from the triangular area beneath the force–extension graph, and the square means a spring stretched twice as far stores four times the energy, not twice. Do not confuse the force F = kx (the height of the graph) with the energy Es = ½kx² (the area beneath it).
sagan.34: A spring launcher has a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego compresses it by 0.080 m, places a 0.20 kg glider against it on a frictionless horizontal track, and releases it. All the stored elastic energy is transferred to the glider as kinetic energy.
sagan.35: Formula: Es = ½kx², then energy conservation Es = Ek = ½mv².
sagan.36: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J. Then ½ × 0.20 × v² = 0.80, so v² = 8.0.
sagan.37: Answer: v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹, directed along the track in the direction the spring pushes.
sagan.38: Energy is never created and never destroyed; it only changes its garments, passing from one form into another. The three mechanical forms gathered in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy, the energy laid up by lifting a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
sagan.39: This expression rings true only in a uniform field — near Earth's surface, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ holds effectively constant across the modest height changes involved. For the far grander height changes of a satellite, g itself drifts, and Eg must instead be found from the area under a force–distance graph, or from the area under a field–distance graph multiplied by the mass (treated in the Fields chapter).
sagan.40: In an isolated system unvisited by dissipative forces, the total mechanical energy — the sum of the kinetic, elastic and gravitational potential energies — holds perfectly constant. A falling ball pours gravitational potential energy into kinetic energy; a launched spring pours elastic potential energy first into kinetic energy and then, as the object climbs, into gravitational potential energy. Following the total is often swifter than wrestling with forces and kinematics, because the winding path between the start and the end states simply does not matter.
sagan.41: A mass hung upon a spring and set oscillating is a ceaseless exchange of energy. At the lowest point the spring is stretched the most, so elastic potential energy stands greatest and kinetic energy is zero; as the mass rises the spring relaxes and the mass gathers speed, so the elastic and gravitational stores spill over into kinetic energy; at the highest point the kinetic energy falls to zero once more. In an ideal system the total holds constant and the oscillation goes on without end; in a real system a little energy slips away each cycle, and the oscillation gradually fades into stillness.
sagan.42: Real systems are never perfectly isolated. Friction, drag and the deformation of colliding bodies dissipate mechanical energy out into the environment as heat, sound and permanent deformation. When dissipation takes its toll the mechanical energy diminishes, yet the total energy — now reckoning in the heat and sound that were born — is still conserved. The energy accounting closes exactly only when these scattered forms are gathered back into the ledger.
sagan.43: Key idea In an isolated system with no dissipation, total mechanical energy (kinetic + elastic + gravitational) is conserved. When friction, drag or deformation act, mechanical energy is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is still conserved once those forms are counted.
sagan.44: The launcher of the previous example is now held vertically. The same spring (k = 250 N m⁻¹) is compressed by 0.080 m and a 0.20 kg ball is placed on top. The ball is released and rises freely. Priya finds the maximum height the ball reaches above its release point, neglecting air resistance, and takes g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
sagan.45: Formula: with no dissipation the elastic potential energy stored becomes gravitational potential energy at the highest point, where the ball is momentarily at rest: Es = mgΔh.
sagan.46: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J, so 0.80 = 0.20 × 9.8 × Δh.
sagan.47: Answer: Δh = 0.80 ÷ (0.20 × 9.8) = 0.41 m above the release point. Air resistance, which dissipates energy as heat and sound, would reduce this height.

Throughout the flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is g downward even while the ball is still rising. Air resistance dissipates energy and lowers both the maximum height and the range.

cox.38: Energy is never created and never destroyed; it only changes from one form into another. The three mechanical forms in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy, the energy stored by lifting a mass through a height in a gravitational field:

greene.0: Forces are what we usually reach for when we want to explain why motion changes — but there is a quieter, more powerful way to think. Nature hands us two conserved quantities, momentum and energy, and with them an entire collision or launch can be unravelled without ever needing to know the force at every passing instant. In what follows we will treat impulse as the area beneath a force–time graph, sort collisions into elastic and inelastic by following the kinetic energy as it goes, and watch energy flow among its kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms before some of it slips away into the environment.
greene.1: Begin with the simplest idea. The momentum of an object is its mass multiplied by its velocity, p = mv. And because velocity is a vector — it points somewhere — momentum inherits that directionality: it points the very same way the velocity does. In one dimension we tame this by choosing a positive direction and letting motion the other way carry a negative sign.
greene.2: A net force is what changes an object's momentum. Newton's second law, written in its most general and most honest form, says that the net force equals the rate at which momentum changes. So across a stretch of time Δt, the net force builds up a change in momentum — a quantity we call the impulse:
greene.3: Impulse and change in momentum are not two things but one thing seen from two angles, which is why 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹. The change in velocity is simply the final velocity less the initial, Δv = v − u, and here the signs matter enormously: a ball that rebounds turns around, so its velocity flips sign, and the impulse can be larger than the one needed merely to bring it to a halt.
greene.4: When the force shifts and swells during the interval, the F in FΔt stands for the average force. The beautiful shortcut is this: the impulse can be read straight off a force–time graph as the area between the graph and the time axis. And it works no matter the shape — a rectangle for a steady force, a triangle for one that climbs and falls in straight lines, a trapezium for a force that ramps up, holds firm, then ramps back down.
greene.5: Trap Momentum is a vector, and forgetting that leads you astray. A 2.0 kg trolley travelling at 3.0 m s⁻¹ that rebounds at 3.0 m s⁻¹ has not undergone zero change in momentum — far from it. Take the original direction as positive: the momentum swings from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always pin + and − to opposite directions, and hold them fixed all the way through a problem.
greene.6: Picture Mei pushing a stationary 1.5 kg trolley along a frictionless track. A force sensor captures the force she applies as the trapezoidal pulse of Figure 4.1: the force climbs linearly from 0 to 40 N over the first 0.10 s, holds steady at 40 N until 0.30 s, then falls away linearly to 0 at 0.40 s. The trolley sits at rest before she begins.
greene.7: Formula: FΔt = mΔv, with the impulse FΔt equal to the area under the force–time graph.
greene.9: Answer: the impulse comes to 12 N s, so Δv = FΔt / m = 12 ÷ 1.5 = 8.0 m s⁻¹. The trolley began at rest, so its final velocity is 8.0 m s⁻¹ in the direction of the push.
greene.10: An isolated system is one on which no net external force acts. The total momentum of such a system never changes: whatever momentum one object gains, another surrenders in exactly equal measure — because the forces the objects press on each other form a Newton's third law pair, equal and opposite, acting for precisely the same time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and what makes it so remarkable is its reach: it holds in every collision and explosion alike, whether the objects deform, stick, bounce or shatter into pieces.
greene.11: In one dimension, once a positive direction is chosen, conservation of momentum takes the form:
greene.12: Kinetic energy is another matter entirely. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy afterward equals the total before — nothing is mislaid. In an inelastic collision the total kinetic energy afterward falls short of what came before; yet that difference is not destroyed, only transformed — into heat, into sound, into the energy locked in permanent deformation. A collision in which the objects fuse together is perfectly inelastic, and it sheds the greatest kinetic energy that conserving momentum will allow.
greene.13: To classify a collision, we lean first on momentum conservation to pin down the unknown velocities, then compute the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² both before and after. If the two totals match, the collision is elastic; if the total afterward is smaller, the collision is inelastic, and that shortfall is precisely the energy dissipated to the environment.
greene.14: Key idea Momentum is conserved in every collision within an isolated system. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. If Ek after is less than Ek before, the collision is inelastic and the missing energy has become heat, sound and deformation.
greene.15: Trap "Energy is lost" never means energy vanishes. Total energy is always conserved — without exception. In an inelastic collision it is the kinetic energy that drops; and that energy is accounted for to the last joule, as heat, as sound, and as the work done permanently deforming the colliding objects.
greene.16: On a frictionless air track, cart A of mass 0.80 kg glides at 3.0 m s⁻¹ to the right and meets a stationary cart B of mass 0.40 kg. The carts couple together and move off as a single body. Sora takes the right as positive and treats the two carts as an isolated system.
greene.18: Substitution: (0.80 × 3.0) + (0.40 × 0) = (0.80 + 0.40) × v, so v = 2.4 ÷ 1.2 = 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right.
greene.19: Formula (classify): compare Ek = ½mv² before and after.
greene.20: Substitution: Ek before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J; Ek after = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J.
greene.21: Answer: the kinetic energy falls from 3.6 J to 2.4 J, so the collision is inelastic — perfectly inelastic, in fact, because the carts stick fast. The 1.2 J difference is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation. Momentum, meanwhile, is untouched: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.
greene.22: Work is the energy handed to an object by a force acting through a displacement. For a constant force pushing along the line of motion, the work done is the force multiplied by the displacement:
greene.23: When the force varies as the object moves, the work done becomes the area under a force–distance graph. This is the very same area rule we used for impulse on a force–time graph, and the one we will soon use for elastic energy on a force–extension graph: in each case the area beneath a force-versus-something graph gathers up the accumulated effect of the force.
greene.24: Only the component of force along the displacement does any work. A force aimed perpendicular to the displacement does zero work, for it has no component in the direction of motion at all. This is the secret behind uniform circular motion keeping a constant speed: the net force points to the centre, the velocity runs tangential, and the two stand at right angles, so the centripetal force pours no energy into the object. It forever turns the object's direction without ever altering its speed.
greene.25: The energy an object holds purely by virtue of its motion is its kinetic energy:
greene.26: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: a net force that does positive work hurries the object along, and one that does negative work drags it down. And because kinetic energy depends on the square of the speed, doubling the speed does not double the energy — it quadruples it.
greene.27: Key idea A force perpendicular to the displacement does zero work. The centripetal force in uniform circular motion is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work and the speed stays constant even though the velocity changes direction.
greene.28: Connects This is why a satellite in a circular orbit, and a car at steady speed on a circular road, keep a constant speed: gravity and friction respectively act toward the centre, perpendicular to the motion, and transfer no energy. The work-as-area rule reappears for springs in the next section.
greene.29: An ideal spring obeys Hooke's law: the force it exerts is proportional to how far it is stretched or compressed. Writing the magnitudes, the applied force needed to hold the spring at extension x is:
greene.30: The spring constant k is the gradient of the force–extension graph: a stiffer spring draws a steeper line. The graph of applied force against extension is therefore a straight line through the origin, with gradient k.
greene.31: The elastic potential energy stored in a stretched or compressed spring is exactly the work done in deforming it — which is, once again, the area under the force–extension graph. Because that graph is a triangle of base x and height kx, its area is ½ × x × kx:
greene.32: The stored energy hangs on the square of the extension. Double the extension and you double the force — but you quadruple the stored energy, because the triangle's area swells with both its base and its height at once.
greene.33: Trap Es = ½kx² is quadratic in x; it is not kx and not ½kx. The factor of ½ comes from the triangular area under the force–extension graph, and the square means a spring stretched twice as far stores four times the energy, not twice. Do not confuse the force F = kx (the height of the graph) with the energy Es = ½kx² (the area under it).
greene.34: A spring launcher has a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego compresses it by 0.080 m, sets a 0.20 kg glider against it on a frictionless horizontal track, and lets go. Every bit of the stored elastic energy passes to the glider as kinetic energy.
greene.35: Formula: Es = ½kx², then energy conservation Es = Ek = ½mv².
greene.36: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J. Then ½ × 0.20 × v² = 0.80, so v² = 8.0.
greene.37: Answer: v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹, directed along the track in the direction the spring pushes.
greene.38: Energy is never conjured into being nor snuffed out of existence; it merely changes its form, slipping from one guise into another. The three mechanical guises in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy, the energy banked by lifting a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
greene.39: This expression holds only in a uniform field — near Earth's surface, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ stays effectively constant across the height changes in play. For the far grander height changes of a satellite, g itself varies, and Eg must instead be drawn from the area under a force–distance graph, or from the area under a field–distance graph multiplied by the mass (a story told in the Fields chapter).
greene.40: In an isolated system free of dissipative forces, the total mechanical energy — the sum of the kinetic, elastic and gravitational potential energies — holds perfectly constant. A falling ball pours gravitational potential energy into kinetic energy; a launched spring pours elastic potential energy first into kinetic energy and then, as the object climbs, into gravitational potential energy. Following the running total is often swifter than wrestling with forces and kinematics, because the path threading the start and end states simply does not matter.
greene.41: A mass hanging on a spring and set oscillating is energy in perpetual exchange. At the lowest point the spring is stretched most, so elastic potential energy peaks and kinetic energy falls to zero; as the mass rises the spring relaxes and the mass gathers speed, so the elastic and gravitational stores pour into kinetic energy; at the highest point the kinetic energy is once again zero. greene.42: No real system is ever perfectly isolated. Friction, drag and the deformation of colliding bodies dissipate mechanical energy out into the environment as heat, sound and permanent deformation. When this happens the mechanical energy declines — but the total energy, now reckoning the heat and sound produced, is still conserved. The books balance to the last joule only when these dissipated forms are folded back in.
greene.43: Key idea In an isolated system with no dissipation, total mechanical energy (kinetic + elastic + gravitational) is conserved. When friction, drag or deformation act, mechanical energy is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is still conserved once those forms are counted.

The ball is released and rises freely into the air. Priya seeks the maximum height the ball reaches above its release point, neglecting air resistance, and takes g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹.

greene.47: Answer: Δh = 0.80 ÷ (0.20 × 9.8) = 0.41 m above the release point. Air resistance, which bleeds energy away as heat and sound, would trim this height.

nye.10: An isolated system is one with no net external force acting on it. And here’s the powerful idea: the total momentum of an isolated system stays constant! Whatever momentum one object gains, another loses by exactly the same amount — because the forces the objects push on each other with are a Newton’s third law pair, acting for the same time. That’s the law of conservation of momentum, and it holds in every single collision and explosion, whether the objects deform, stick, bounce or shatter!

nye.12: Now kinetic energy is a whole different story. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy after equals the total before. In an inelastic collision the total kinetic energy after is less than before — but that difference isn’t destroyed! And a collision where the objects stick together is perfectly inelastic — it loses the greatest possible kinetic energy you can lose while still conserving momentum.
nye.13: So how do you classify a collision? Use momentum conservation first to find the unknown velocities, then work out the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² before and after. The 1.2 J difference gets dissipated out to the environment as heat, sound and deformation. But momentum? Momentum is conserved: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.

It just keeps changing the object’s direction without ever changing its speed.

nye.34: Here’s a great one: a spring launcher has a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego compresses it by 0.080 m, sets a 0.20 kg glider against it on a frictionless horizontal track, and lets it go. All the stored elastic energy gets transferred to the glider as kinetic energy.

nye.38: Energy is never created and never destroyed — it just transforms from one form into another! bronowski.0: Forces change motion from instant to instant, and to follow them so closely is exhausting work. Yet nature has left us two quantities that survive untouched through the whole of a collision or a launch — momentum and energy — and through them an entire event can be reckoned without ever knowing the force at every moment. This page reads impulse as the area beneath a force–time graph, sorts collisions into elastic and inelastic by following where the kinetic energy goes, and traces energy as it changes among its kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms and slips away into the environment.

A falling ball converts gravitational potential energy into kinetic energy; a launched spring converts elastic potential energy first into kinetic energy and then, as the object rises, into gravitational potential energy — the forms trade among themselves while their sum holds fixed. Following that conserved sum is often faster than chasing forces and kinematics, because the path between the start and end states simply does not matter.
noether.41: A mass hanging on a spring and set oscillating is a continuous exchange among the energy stores. At the lowest point the spring is most stretched, so elastic potential energy is greatest and kinetic energy is zero; as the mass rises the spring relaxes and the mass speeds up, so the elastic and gravitational stores pour into kinetic energy; at the highest point the kinetic energy is once more zero. In an ideal system the total is preserved and the oscillation runs forever; in a real system a little energy escapes each cycle, the total mechanical store dwindles, and the oscillation gradually dies away.
noether.42: Real systems are never perfectly isolated. Friction, drag and the deformation of colliding bodies dissipate mechanical energy to the environment as heat, sound and permanent deformation. Where dissipation acts, the mechanical energy decreases — but the total energy, once the heat and sound are counted in, is still conserved. The deeper invariant never breaks; the accounting closes exactly only when these dissipated forms are admitted to the ledger.
noether.43: Key idea In an isolated system with no dissipation, total mechanical energy (kinetic + elastic + gravitational) is conserved. When friction, drag or deformation act, mechanical energy is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is still conserved once those forms are counted.

Air resistance dissipates energy and lowers both the maximum height and the range.

mack.0: Forces are what change motion — but chasing the force at every single instant of a collision is a nightmare you can usually skip. Two quantities refuse to go missing, momentum and energy, and that lets you analyse an entire smash-up or launch without ever knowing the force moment by moment. This page reads impulse straight off the area under a force–time graph, sorts collisions into elastic or inelastic by keeping an eye on the kinetic energy, and follows energy as it slides between kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms — and leaks out to the environment.
mack.1: The momentum of an object is just its mass times its velocity, p = mv. And because velocity is a vector, momentum inherits that: it points the same way the velocity does. In one dimension you tame this the easy way — pick a positive direction and call anything heading the other way negative.
mack.2: Hit an object with a net force and you change its momentum. Newton's second law, in its full-strength form, says the net force equals the rate at which momentum changes — so over a stretch of time Δt that net force racks up a change in momentum we call the impulse:
mack.3: Impulse and change in momentum are the same thing wearing two different name tags, which is why 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹. The change in velocity is the final minus the initial, Δv = v − u, and you have to babysit the signs: a ball that bounces back has flipped direction, so its velocity changes sign and the impulse can come out bigger than the impulse it would take to merely stop the thing dead.
mack.4: When the force wobbles during the interval, the F in FΔt is the average force. The neat trick is that you can read the impulse straight off a force–time graph as the area between the graph and the time axis. This works no matter what shape the graph takes: a rectangle for a steady force, a triangle for a force that rises and falls in straight lines, or a trapezium for one that ramps up, holds, and ramps back down.
mack.5: Trap Momentum is a vector — never forget it. A 2.0 kg trolley rolling at 3.0 m s⁻¹ that rebounds at 3.0 m s⁻¹ has not undergone zero change in momentum, tempting as that looks. Take the original direction as positive and the momentum swings from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always pin + and − to opposite directions and stay consistent right through the problem.
mack.6: Mei shoves a stationary 1.5 kg trolley along a frictionless track. A force sensor catches the force she applies as the trapezoidal pulse in Figure 4.1: the force climbs in a straight line from 0 to 40 N over the first 0.10 s, sits at 40 N until 0.30 s, then drops linearly back to 0 at 0.40 s. The trolley is at rest before she touches it.
mack.7: Formula: FΔt = mΔv, with the impulse FΔt equal to the area under the force–time graph.
mack.9: Answer: the impulse is 12 N s, so Δv = FΔt / m = 12 ÷ 1.5 = 8.0 m s⁻¹. The trolley started from rest, so its final velocity is 8.0 m s⁻¹ in the direction of the push.
mack.10: An isolated system is one with no net external force leaning on it. The total momentum of an isolated system simply doesn't change: whatever momentum one object pockets, another hands over by exactly the same amount — because the forces the objects push on each other with are a Newton's third law pair, acting for the same time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and here's the beautiful part: it holds in every collision and explosion, whether the objects deform, stick, bounce or shatter into confetti.
mack.11: In one dimension, with a positive direction chosen, conservation of momentum is written:
mack.12: Kinetic energy is a different story entirely. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy afterwards matches the total before, to the joule. In an inelastic collision the total kinetic energy afterwards comes up short; that missing energy isn't destroyed — it's been quietly repackaged into other forms: heat, sound and the energy of permanent deformation. A collision where the objects stick together is perfectly inelastic, and it sheds the most kinetic energy you can possibly lose while still conserving momentum.
mack.13: To pin down what kind of collision you've got, lean on momentum conservation first to dig out the unknown velocities, then add up the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² before and after. If the two totals match, the collision is elastic; if the total afterwards is smaller, it's inelastic, and the shortfall is exactly the energy that's been dissipated to the environment.
mack.14: Key idea Momentum is conserved in every collision in an isolated system. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. If Ek after is less than Ek before, the collision is inelastic and the missing energy has gone off to become heat, sound and deformation.
mack.15: Trap "Energy is lost" never means energy vanishes into thin air. Total energy is always conserved. In an inelastic collision it's the kinetic energy that drops; that energy is accounted for to the last joule as heat, sound and the work spent permanently bending the colliding objects out of shape.
mack.16: On a frictionless air track, cart A of mass 0.80 kg glides along at 3.0 m s⁻¹ to the right and slams into a stationary cart B of mass 0.40 kg. The carts couple together and move off as a single unit. Sora takes the right as positive and treats the two carts as an isolated system.
mack.18: Substitution: (0.80 × 3.0) + (0.40 × 0) = (0.80 + 0.40) × v, so v = 2.4 ÷ 1.2 = 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right.
mack.19: Formula (classify): compare Ek = ½mv² before and after.
mack.20: Substitution: Ek before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J; Ek after = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J.
mack.21: Answer: the kinetic energy slumps from 3.6 J to 2.4 J, so the collision is inelastic — perfectly inelastic, in fact, because the carts stick. The 1.2 J difference is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation. Momentum, on the other hand, doesn't budge: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.
mack.22: Work is the energy a force pumps into an object by acting through a displacement. For a constant force pushing along the line of motion, the work done is just the force times the displacement:
mack.23: When the force changes with position, the work done is the area under a force–distance graph. It's the very same area rule you used for impulse on a force–time graph and the one coming up for elastic energy on a force–extension graph: in every case, the area of a force-versus-something graph hands you the total accumulated effect of the force.
mack.24: Only the slice of force pointing along the displacement does any work. A force aimed perpendicular to the displacement does zero work, because it has no component in the direction of motion at all. This is exactly why the speed in uniform circular motion stays put: the net force aims at the centre, the velocity runs tangential, the two sit at right angles, so the centripetal force never transfers a scrap of energy to the object. It endlessly swings the object's direction around without ever nudging its speed.
mack.25: The energy an object carries simply by being in motion is its kinetic energy:
mack.26: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: a net force that does positive work speeds the object up, and one that does negative work drags it down. And because kinetic energy rides on the square of the speed, doubling the speed quadruples the kinetic energy.
mack.27: Key idea A force perpendicular to the displacement does zero work. The centripetal force in uniform circular motion is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work and the speed holds steady even as the velocity keeps swinging its direction around.
mack.28: Connects This is why a satellite in a circular orbit, and a car cruising at steady speed on a circular road, both keep a constant speed: gravity and friction respectively pull toward the centre, perpendicular to the motion, and transfer no energy. The work-as-area rule makes its return for springs in the next section.

mack.30: The spring constant k is the gradient of the force–extension graph: a stiffer spring gives a steeper line. So the graph of applied force against extension is a straight line through the origin, with gradient k.
mack.31: The elastic potential energy bottled up in a stretched or compressed spring is the work you did deforming it, which is the area under the force–extension graph. Since that graph is a triangle of base x and height kx, the area comes out as ½ × x × kx:
mack.32: The stored energy hangs on the square of the extension. Stretch it twice as far and you double the force but quadruple the stored energy — because the triangle's area grows along both its base and its height at once.
mack.33: Trap Es = ½kx² is quadratic in x; it is not kx and it is not ½kx. The factor of ½ falls out of the triangular area under the force–extension graph, and the square means a spring pulled twice as far stores four times the energy, not twice. Don't muddle the force F = kx (the height of the graph) with the energy Es = ½kx² (the area under it).
mack.34: A spring launcher has a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego squashes it by 0.080 m, sets a 0.20 kg glider against it on a frictionless horizontal track, and lets go. All the stored elastic energy pours into the glider as kinetic energy.
mack.35: Formula: Es = ½kx², then energy conservation Es = Ek = ½mv².

mack.37: Answer: v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹, headed along the track in the direction the spring pushes.
mack.38: Energy is never made and never unmade; it only morphs from one form into another. The three mechanical forms in play here are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy — the energy you bank by hoisting a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
mack.39: This expression only holds in a uniform field — near Earth's surface, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ stays effectively constant over the height changes you're dealing with. For the far bigger height changes of a satellite, g itself drifts, and Eg has to come instead from the area under a force–distance graph, or from the area under a field–distance graph multiplied by the mass (that's the Fields chapter's territory).

At the lowest point the spring is stretched hardest, so elastic potential energy peaks and kinetic energy is zero; as the mass climbs, the spring relaxes and the mass picks up speed, so the elastic and gravitational stores feed into kinetic energy; at the highest point the kinetic energy is back to zero. When that happens the mechanical energy drops, but the total energy — now counting the heat and sound spawned along the way — is still conserved. The energy books only balance to the penny once you've tallied up those dissipated forms.

When friction, drag or deformation get involved, mechanical energy is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is still conserved once those forms are counted.

It doesn't matter what shape that area is: a rectangle for a constant force, a triangle for a force that climbs and falls in straight lines, or a trapezium for one that ramps up, holds steady and ramps down again.

Call the original direction positive and the momentum swings from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always pin + and − onto opposite directions and stick with that choice all the way through a problem.
czerski.6: Mei gives a stationary 1.5 kg trolley a shove along a frictionless track. A force sensor records the push she applies as the trapezoidal pulse of Figure 4.1: the force climbs in a straight line from 0 to 40 N over the first 0.10 s, sits at 40 N until 0.30 s, then drops back to 0 at 0.40 s. The trolley is at rest before she touches it.

czerski.10: An isolated system is one with no net external force leaning on it. The total momentum of such a system never changes: whatever momentum one object picks up, another gives up by precisely the same amount, because the forces the objects press on each other are a Newton's third law pair, equal and opposite and lasting exactly the same time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and the beautiful thing is that it holds in every collision and explosion you can dream up — whether the objects crumple, stick, bounce or fly apart in pieces.
czerski.11: In one dimension, once you've chosen a positive direction, conservation of momentum reads:
czerski.12: Kinetic energy is a different beast altogether. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy afterwards matches the total before, to the joule. In an inelastic collision there's less kinetic energy after than before; that missing energy hasn't been destroyed — it's been turned into other things: heat, sound, and the energy locked up in a permanent dent. A collision where the objects stick together is perfectly inelastic, and it sheds the most kinetic energy it possibly can while still keeping momentum on the books.
czerski.13: To pin down which kind of collision you've got, reach for momentum conservation first to find the velocities you don't know, then add up the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² before and after. If the two totals come out equal the collision is elastic; if the total afterwards is smaller it's inelastic, and the shortfall is exactly the energy that has drained away into the surroundings.
czerski.14: Key idea Momentum is conserved in every collision in an isolated system. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. If Ek after is less than Ek before, the collision is inelastic and the missing energy has turned into heat, sound and deformation.
czerski.15: Trap "Energy is lost" never means energy has vanished. Total energy is always conserved — full stop. In an inelastic collision it's the kinetic energy that drops; and every joule of it can be accounted for, as heat, sound, and the work spent permanently buckling the objects that smashed together.
czerski.16: On a frictionless air track, cart A of mass 0.80 kg glides along at 3.0 m s⁻¹ to the right and runs into a stationary cart B of mass 0.40 kg. The two couple together and trundle off as one. Sora calls the right positive and treats the pair of carts as an isolated system.
czerski.18: Substitution: (0.80 × 3.0) + (0.40 × 0) = (0.80 + 0.40) × v, so v = 2.4 ÷ 1.2 = 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right.
czerski.19: Formula (classify): compare Ek = ½mv² before and after.
czerski.20: Substitution: Ek before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J; Ek after = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J.
czerski.21: Answer: the kinetic energy falls from 3.6 J to 2.4 J, so the collision is inelastic — in fact perfectly inelastic, because the carts lock together. The 1.2 J that goes missing drains into the surroundings as heat, sound and deformation. Momentum, though, holds firm: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.

czerski.23: When the force changes as the object moves, the work done is the area under a force–distance graph. It's the very same area trick we used for impulse on a force–time graph, and the one we'll use for elastic energy on a force–extension graph: every time, the area of a force-versus-something graph hands you the total built-up effect of that force.
czerski.24: Only the slice of force pointing along the displacement does any work. A force aimed perpendicular to the displacement does zero work, because it has no part of itself pointing the way the object is moving. This is exactly why the speed in uniform circular motion stays put: the net force points to the centre, the velocity points along the tangent, the two sit at right angles, and so the centripetal force pours no energy into the object at all. It keeps swinging the object's direction round and round without ever nudging its speed.
czerski.25: The energy an object carries simply because it's moving is its kinetic energy:
czerski.26: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: a net force doing positive work speeds the object up, and one doing negative work drags it down. And because kinetic energy goes with the square of the speed, double the speed and you quadruple the kinetic energy.

czerski.28: Connects This is why a satellite in a circular orbit, and a car holding a steady speed round a circular road, both keep that speed: gravity and friction respectively pull toward the centre, square across the motion, and hand over no energy. The work-as-area rule turns up again for springs in the next section.

czerski.30: The spring constant k is the gradient of the force–extension graph: a stiffer spring gives a steeper line. So the graph of applied force against extension is a straight line marching up from the origin, with gradient k.

czerski.32: The energy you've stored depends on the square of the extension. Stretch it twice as far and you double the force but quadruple the stored energy, because the triangle's area grows with its base and its height at once.
czerski.33: Trap Es = ½kx² is quadratic in x; it is not kx and it is not ½kx. That factor of ½ comes straight from the triangular area under the force–extension graph, and the square means a spring stretched twice as far stores four times the energy, not twice. Don't muddle the force F = kx (the height of the graph) with the energy Es = ½kx² (the area beneath it).
czerski.34: A spring launcher has a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego squashes it down by 0.080 m, sets a 0.20 kg glider against it on a frictionless horizontal track, and lets go. Every last bit of the stored elastic energy passes into the glider as kinetic energy.
czerski.35: Formula: Es = ½kx², then energy conservation Es = Ek = ½mv².
czerski.36: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J. Then ½ × 0.20 × v² = 0.80, so v² = 8.0.
czerski.37: Answer: v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹, headed along the track the way the spring pushes.
czerski.38: Energy is never made and never destroyed; it only changes its costume, slipping from one form into another. The three mechanical wardrobes in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy, the energy you bank by lifting a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
czerski.39: This expression only holds in a uniform field — close to Earth's surface, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ stays effectively rock-steady over the height changes you're dealing with. For the far grander climbs of a satellite, g itself changes, and Eg has to be dug out from the area under a force–distance graph instead, or from the area under a field–distance graph multiplied by the mass (we tackle that in the Fields chapter).
czerski.40: In an isolated system with no dissipative forces nibbling away, the total mechanical energy — the kinetic, elastic and gravitational potential energies all added together — stays constant. A falling ball pours gravitational potential energy into kinetic energy; a launched spring tips elastic potential energy first into kinetic energy and then, as the object climbs, into gravitational potential energy. Following the running total is often far quicker than wrestling with forces and kinematics, because the route taken between the start and the finish simply doesn't matter.
czerski.41: A mass dangling on a spring and set bobbing is a never-ending swap of energy. At the lowest point the spring is stretched the most, so elastic potential energy is at its peak and kinetic energy is nil; as the mass rises the spring eases off and the mass picks up speed, so the elastic and gravitational stores feed into kinetic energy; at the highest point the kinetic energy drops back to zero. In an ideal world the total holds steady and the bobbing goes on forever; in the real world a little energy leaks away each cycle and the oscillation gently winds down to nothing.
czerski.42: Real systems are never perfectly sealed off. Friction, drag and the crumpling of colliding bodies dissipate mechanical energy out into the surroundings as heat, sound and permanent deformation. When that happens the mechanical energy shrinks — but the total energy, now counting the heat and sound that got made, is still conserved. The books only balance to the last joule once you've added those leaked-away forms back in.
czerski.43: Key idea In an isolated system with no dissipation, total mechanical energy (kinetic + elastic + gravitational) is conserved. When friction, drag or deformation act, mechanical energy is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is still conserved once those forms are counted.
czerski.44: The launcher from the last example now stands upright. The same spring (k = 250 N m⁻¹) is squashed by 0.080 m and a 0.20 kg ball is balanced on top. The ball is let go and shoots up freely. Priya works out the greatest height the ball reaches above where it started, ignoring air resistance, and takes g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹.

czerski.47: Answer: Δh = 0.80 ÷ (0.20 × 9.8) = 0.41 m above the release point. Air resistance, which bleeds energy off as heat and sound, would trim this height down.
czerski.48: Connects The very same energy bookkeeping runs a projectile: a ball thrown upward trades kinetic energy for gravitational potential energy on the way up and trades it straight back on the way down, hitting the same speed at any given height (so long as there's no air resistance). All through the flight the only force at work is gravity, so the acceleration is g downward even while the ball is still climbing. Air resistance bleeds energy away and pulls down both the maximum height and the range.

veritasium.0: You'd think that to predict where a collision ends up, or how fast a launched object flies off, you'd need to track the force at every single instant. You don't. Forces change motion, but two quantities sneak through any collision or launch unchanged — momentum and energy — and they let you solve the whole thing without ever knowing the force moment by moment. This page reads impulse straight off the area under a force–time graph, sorts collisions into elastic or inelastic by following the kinetic energy, and tracks energy as it shape-shifts among kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms and leaks out to the environment.
veritasium.1: The momentum of an object is just its mass times its velocity, p = mv. Here's the part people skip over: because velocity is a vector, momentum is a vector too — it points the same way the velocity does. In one dimension you handle that by picking a positive direction and calling motion the other way negative.
veritasium.2: A net force changes an object's momentum. You probably learned Newton's second law as F = ma, but its more general form says the net force equals the rate of change of momentum. So over a time interval Δt the net force delivers a change in momentum we call the impulse:
veritasium.3: Impulse and change in momentum aren't two different things — they're one quantity measured two ways, which is exactly why 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹. The change in velocity is final minus initial, Δv = v − u, and this is where signs will catch you out: a ball that rebounds reverses direction, so its velocity flips sign, and the impulse to bounce it back can be larger than the impulse it took just to stop it.
veritasium.4: When the force varies during the interval, the F in FΔt is the average force. But you don't need the average — you can read the impulse straight off a force–time graph as the area between the graph and the time axis. And this works no matter what shape the graph is: a rectangle for a constant force, a triangle for a force that rises and falls linearly, or a trapezium for a force that ramps up, holds steady and ramps back down.
veritasium.5: Trap Momentum is a vector, and that trips almost everyone here. A 2.0 kg trolley moving at 3.0 m s⁻¹ that rebounds at 3.0 m s⁻¹ has not undergone zero change in momentum, even though the speeds match. Take the original direction as positive: the momentum goes from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always assign + and − to opposite directions and keep them consistent through the whole problem.

The trolley is at rest before the push.
veritasium.7: Formula: FΔt = mΔv, with the impulse FΔt equal to the area under the force–time graph.

veritasium.10: An isolated system is one with no net external force acting on it. Here's the claim that sounds too strong to be true: the total momentum of an isolated system never changes. Whatever momentum one object gains, another loses by exactly the same amount — and not by coincidence, but because the forces the objects exert on each other are a Newton's third law pair, equal and opposite, acting for the same time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and it holds in every collision and explosion, whether the objects deform, stick, bounce or shatter.

veritasium.12: Kinetic energy is a different story, and this is where intuition leads you astray. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In an inelastic collision the total afterward is less than before — but that missing energy isn't destroyed, it's transformed into heat, sound and the energy of permanent deformation. A collision in which the objects stick together is perfectly inelastic and loses the most kinetic energy it possibly can while still conserving momentum.
veritasium.13: So how do you classify a collision? Use momentum conservation first to pin down the unknown velocities, then compute the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² before and after. If the two totals match, the collision is elastic; if the total after is smaller, it's inelastic, and the shortfall is precisely the energy dissipated to the environment.

veritasium.38: Energy is never created or destroyed; it only changes form. The three mechanical forms in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy — the energy you store by raising a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
veritasium.39: Be careful: this expression is valid only in a uniform field — near Earth's surface, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ is effectively constant over the height changes involved.
