attenborough.0: Forces change motion — but nature, in her economy, has hidden two quantities that survive the chaos. Momentum and energy: two things that endure, that let us follow an entire collision, an entire launch, without ever needing to know the force at each fleeting instant. Here we shall watch impulse emerge as the area beneath a force–time graph, sort collisions into the elastic and the inelastic by following the kinetic energy wherever it goes, and trace energy as it flows between its kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms, before it slips quietly away into the environment.
attenborough.1: The momentum of an object is the product of its mass and its velocity, p = mv. And because velocity is a vector — a quantity with a direction — momentum, too, is a vector, pointing always the way the velocity points. In one dimension we tame it simply: we choose a positive direction, and motion the other way we call negative.
attenborough.2: A net force changes an object's momentum. Newton's second law, in its fullest and most general form, tells us that the net force is the rate of change of momentum — and so, across a span of time Δt, the net force works a change in momentum that we call the impulse:
attenborough.3: Impulse and change in momentum are but one quantity, glimpsed two ways, and so 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹. The change in velocity is the final velocity less the initial, Δv = v − u, and the signs must be watched with care: a ball that rebounds turns and flees the way it came, so its velocity changes sign — and the impulse can be greater, far greater, than the impulse needed merely to bring it to rest.
attenborough.4: When the force shifts and wavers through the interval, the F in FΔt is the average force. The impulse may be read straight from a force–time graph as the area between the graph and the time axis. And this holds true whatever shape the graph may take: a rectangle for a steady force, a triangle for one that rises and falls in a clean line, a trapezium for a force that climbs, holds, and gently subsides.
attenborough.5: Trap Momentum is a vector — never forget it. A 2.0 kg trolley moving at 3.0 m s⁻¹ that rebounds at 3.0 m s⁻¹ has not, as it may seem, undergone zero change in momentum. Take the original direction as positive: the momentum swings from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always give + and − to opposite directions, and hold to them, faithfully, throughout the whole of a problem.
attenborough.6: Mei pushes a stationary 1.5 kg trolley along a frictionless track. A force sensor records the force she applies as the trapezoidal pulse of Figure 4.1: the force rises linearly from 0 to 40 N over the first 0.10 s, holds at 40 N until 0.30 s, then falls linearly to 0 at 0.40 s. The trolley is at rest before the push.
attenborough.7: Formula: FΔt = mΔv, with the impulse FΔt equal to the area under the force–time graph.
attenborough.9: Answer: the impulse is 12 N s, so Δv = FΔt / m = 12 ÷ 1.5 = 8.0 m s⁻¹. The trolley started at rest, so its final velocity is 8.0 m s⁻¹ in the direction of the push.
attenborough.10: An isolated system is one upon which no net external force acts — a small world left to itself. The total momentum of such a system never changes: whatever momentum one object gains, another surrenders, to the very last unit, for the forces the objects press upon one another are a Newton's third law pair, acting for precisely the same time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and it holds in every collision and explosion that has ever been — whether the objects deform, stick, bounce or shatter into pieces.
attenborough.11: In one dimension, with a positive direction chosen, conservation of momentum is written:
attenborough.12: Kinetic energy is another matter entirely. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy afterward is exactly the total before — nothing lost. In an inelastic collision the total afterward is less than before; yet that difference is not destroyed, never destroyed, but transformed into other forms — into heat, into sound, into the energy locked in permanent deformation. And a collision in which the objects cling together is perfectly inelastic, surrendering the greatest kinetic energy that conserving momentum will permit.
attenborough.13: To classify a collision, we turn first to conservation of momentum to uncover the unknown velocities, and then we reckon the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², before and after. If the two totals match, the collision is elastic; if the total afterward is the smaller, the collision is inelastic — and the shortfall is the energy that has slipped away into the environment.
attenborough.14: Key idea Momentum is conserved in every collision in an isolated system. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. If Ek after is less than Ek before, the collision is inelastic, and the missing energy has become heat, sound and deformation — nothing of it lost.
attenborough.15: Trap "Energy is lost" never means energy vanishes. Total energy is always, always conserved. In an inelastic collision it is the kinetic energy that falls; and that energy is accounted for, to the last joule, as heat, sound and the work spent permanently deforming the colliding bodies.
attenborough.16: On a frictionless air track, cart A of mass 0.80 kg moves at 3.0 m s⁻¹ to the right and collides with a stationary cart B of mass 0.40 kg. The carts couple together and move off as one. Sora takes the right as positive and treats the two carts as an isolated system.
attenborough.18: Substitution: (0.80 × 3.0) + (0.40 × 0) = (0.80 + 0.40) × v, so v = 2.4 ÷ 1.2 = 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right.
attenborough.19: Formula (classify): compare Ek = ½mv² before and after.
attenborough.20: Substitution: Ek before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J; Ek after = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J.
attenborough.21: Answer: the kinetic energy falls from 3.6 J to 2.4 J, so the collision is inelastic (in fact perfectly inelastic, because the carts stick). The 1.2 J difference is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation. Momentum, however, is conserved: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.
attenborough.22: Work is the energy a force passes to an object as it acts through a displacement. For a constant force acting along the very line of motion, the work done is simply the force multiplied by the displacement:
attenborough.23: When the force changes from place to place, the work done is the area under a force–distance graph. It is the same rule we met for impulse on a force–time graph, and the same we shall meet again for elastic energy on a force–extension graph: in each, the area beneath a force-versus-something graph gathers up the whole accumulated effect of the force.
attenborough.24: Only the part of the force lying along the displacement does any work. A force pointing perpendicular to the displacement does zero work, for it has no share in the direction of motion. And here is the reason that speed in uniform circular motion never changes: the net force points forever to the centre, the velocity slides past it tangentially, the two held at right angles — so the centripetal force gives the object no energy at all. It turns the object's direction, ceaselessly, without ever touching its speed.
attenborough.25: The energy an object holds simply by virtue of its motion is its kinetic energy:
attenborough.26: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: a net force that does positive work hurries the object along, and one that does negative work brings it down. And because kinetic energy rests upon the square of the speed, doubling the speed does not double the energy — it multiplies it fourfold.
attenborough.27: Key idea A force perpendicular to the displacement does zero work. The centripetal force in uniform circular motion is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work, and the speed stays constant even as the velocity wheels endlessly around in direction.
attenborough.28: Connects This is why a satellite in a circular orbit, and a car at steady speed on a circular road, keep a constant speed: gravity and friction respectively act toward the centre, perpendicular to the motion, and transfer no energy. The work-as-area rule reappears for springs in the next section.
attenborough.29: An ideal spring keeps faithfully to Hooke's law: the force it exerts is proportional to how far it is stretched or compressed. Writing the magnitudes, the applied force needed to hold the spring at extension x is:
attenborough.30: The spring constant k is the gradient of the force–extension graph: the stiffer the spring, the steeper the line. And so the graph of applied force against extension is a straight line rising through the origin, of gradient k.
attenborough.31: The elastic potential energy stored in a stretched or compressed spring is the work spent in deforming it — which is the area under the force–extension graph. And because that graph is a triangle of base x and height kx, the area is ½ × x × kx:
attenborough.32: The stored energy rests upon the square of the extension. Stretch the spring twice as far and you double the force — but you quadruple the stored energy, for the triangle's area grows with its base and its height together.
attenborough.33: Trap Es = ½kx² is quadratic in x; it is not kx and not ½kx. The factor of ½ comes from the triangular area under the force–extension graph, and the square means a spring stretched twice as far stores four times the energy, not twice. Do not confuse the force F = kx (the height of the graph) with the energy Es = ½kx² (the area under it).
attenborough.34: A spring launcher has a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego compresses it by 0.080 m, places a 0.20 kg glider against it on a frictionless horizontal track, and releases it. All the stored elastic energy is transferred to the glider as kinetic energy.
attenborough.35: Formula: Es = ½kx², then energy conservation Es = Ek = ½mv².
attenborough.36: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J. Then ½ × 0.20 × v² = 0.80, so v² = 8.0.
attenborough.37: Answer: v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹, directed along the track in the direction the spring pushes.
attenborough.38: Energy is never created, never destroyed; it is only transformed, passing from one form into another. The three mechanical forms in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy — the energy laid up by lifting a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
attenborough.39: This expression holds true only in a uniform field — near the surface of the Earth, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ is as good as constant across the heights we deal with here. For the far greater climbs of a satellite, g itself begins to vary, and Eg must be sought instead from the area under a force–distance graph, or from the area under a field–distance graph multiplied by the mass (a matter taken up in the Fields chapter).
attenborough.40: In an isolated system, with no dissipative forces at work, the total mechanical energy — the sum of the kinetic, the elastic and the gravitational potential energies — stays forever constant. A falling ball turns gravitational potential energy into kinetic energy; a spring, released, turns its elastic potential energy first into kinetic energy, and then, as the object climbs, into gravitational potential energy once more. To follow the total is often swifter than wrestling with forces and kinematics — for the path travelled between the start and the end matters not at all.
attenborough.41: A mass hung upon a spring and set oscillating is a ceaseless exchange of energy. At the lowest point the spring is stretched the most, so elastic potential energy is at its greatest and the kinetic energy is nothing at all; as the mass rises the spring eases and the mass gathers speed, the elastic and gravitational stores pouring into kinetic energy; and at the highest point the kinetic energy is, once again, zero. In an ideal world the total holds constant and the oscillation goes on without end; but in a real one a little energy slips away each cycle, and the swinging, slowly, quietly, dies away.
attenborough.42: Real systems are never perfectly isolated. Friction, drag, and the deformation of colliding bodies dissipate mechanical energy into the environment as heat, sound and permanent deformation. When dissipation comes the mechanical energy falls — yet the total energy, now counting the heat and sound it has made, is conserved still. The books of energy balance to the last joule only when these scattered forms are gathered in.
attenborough.43: Key idea In an isolated system with no dissipation, total mechanical energy (kinetic + elastic + gravitational) is conserved. When friction, drag or deformation act, mechanical energy is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation — and the total energy is conserved still, once those scattered forms are counted in.
attenborough.44: The launcher of the previous example is now held vertically. The same spring (k = 250 N m⁻¹) is compressed by 0.080 m and a 0.20 kg ball is placed on top. The ball is released and rises freely. Priya finds the maximum height the ball reaches above its release point, neglecting air resistance, and takes g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
attenborough.45: Formula: with no dissipation the elastic potential energy stored becomes gravitational potential energy at the highest point, where the ball is momentarily at rest: Es = mgΔh.
attenborough.46: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J, so 0.80 = 0.20 × 9.8 × Δh.
attenborough.47: Answer: Δh = 0.80 ÷ (0.20 × 9.8) = 0.41 m above the release point. Air resistance, which dissipates energy as heat and sound, would reduce this height.
attenborough.48: Connects The same energy bookkeeping governs a projectile: a ball thrown upward trades kinetic energy for gravitational potential energy on the way up and back on the way down, reaching the same speed at any given height (in the absence of air resistance). Throughout the flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is g downward even while the ball is rising. Air resistance dissipates energy and lowers both the maximum height and the range.

greekepic.0: Sing, Muse, of the things that move and strike: for forces do bend the courses of bodies, yet two undying quantities — momentum and energy — suffer no change, and by their grace an entire collision or launch may be reckoned without ever knowing the force at each fleeting instant. This song treats impulse as the area beneath a force–time graph, divides collisions into the elastic and the inelastic by following where the kinetic energy goes, and tracks energy as it is transfigured among its kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms, and dissipates at last into the keeping of the world.
greekepic.1: The momentum of a body is the wedding of its mass to its velocity, p = mv. And because velocity is a vector, so too is momentum a vector, bearing the selfsame heading as the velocity. In one dimension this is mastered by anointing one direction as positive, and reckoning all motion the other way as negative.
greekepic.2: A net force works a change upon a body's momentum. Newton's second law, in its fullest and most ancient form, decrees that the net force is equal to the rate of change of momentum; and so across a span of time Δt the net force begets a change in momentum, which is named the impulse:
greekepic.3: Impulse and change in momentum are one quantity beheld in two manners, and therefore 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹. The change in velocity is the final velocity less the initial, Δv = v − u, and the signs must be guarded with vigilance: a ball that rebounds turns its course about, so its velocity changes sign, and the impulse may be greater than the impulse needed merely to bring it to rest.
greekepic.4: When the force shifts and wavers through the interval, the F in FΔt is the average force. The impulse is read straight from a force–time graph as the area between the graph and the time axis. And this holds true whatever the figure the graph may take: a rectangle for a force that holds constant, a triangle for a force that rises and falls in even measure, or a trapezium for a force that climbs, abides steady, and descends.
greekepic.5: Trap Mark well: momentum is a vector. A 2.0 kg trolley faring at 3.0 m s⁻¹ that rebounds at 3.0 m s⁻¹ has by no means suffered zero change in momentum. Taking the first direction as positive, the momentum is altered from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, and so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always anoint + and − to opposing directions, and keep faith with them throughout the whole of a problem.
greekepic.6: Mei sets her hands to a stationary 1.5 kg trolley and drives it along a frictionless track. A force sensor records the force she wields as the trapezoidal pulse of Figure 4.1: the force climbs in even measure from 0 to 40 N across the first 0.10 s, abides at 40 N until 0.30 s, then descends in even measure to 0 at 0.40 s. The trolley lay at rest before ever the push began.
greekepic.7: Formula: FΔt = mΔv, with the impulse FΔt equal to the area beneath the force–time graph.
greekepic.9: Answer: the impulse is 12 N s, and so Δv = FΔt / m = 12 ÷ 1.5 = 8.0 m s⁻¹. The trolley set forth from rest, and therefore its final velocity is 8.0 m s⁻¹, in the very direction of the push.
greekepic.10: An isolated system is one upon which no net external force lays its hand. The total momentum of such a system stands unchanging: whatsoever momentum one body gains, another surrenders in exactly equal measure, for the forces the bodies wield upon each other are a Newton's third law pair, acting through the selfsame span of time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and it keeps its faith in every collision and explosion, whether the bodies deform or stick, rebound or shatter into pieces.
greekepic.11: In one dimension, a positive direction having been chosen, conservation of momentum is set down thus:
greekepic.12: Kinetic energy is another matter altogether. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy after equals the total before. In an inelastic collision the total kinetic energy after is less than before; yet the difference is not slain — it is transfigured into other forms: heat, sound, and the energy of lasting deformation. A collision in which the bodies cleave together is perfectly inelastic, and surrenders the greatest kinetic energy that may be lost while momentum is yet conserved.
greekepic.13: To name a collision rightly, momentum conservation is wielded first to seek out the unknown velocities; then the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² is reckoned before and after. If the two totals stand equal, the collision is elastic; if the total after is the smaller, the collision is inelastic, and the shortfall is the energy dissipated into the keeping of the world.
greekepic.14: Key idea Momentum is conserved in every collision within an isolated system. Kinetic energy is conserved in an elastic collision alone. If Ek after be less than Ek before, the collision is inelastic, and the missing energy has become heat, sound and deformation.
greekepic.15: Trap Mark well: “energy is lost” never means energy vanishes from the world. Total energy is forever conserved. In an inelastic collision it is the kinetic energy that wanes; and that energy is accounted for to the last, as heat, sound, and the work done in deforming the colliding bodies past mending.
greekepic.16: Upon a frictionless air track, cart A of mass 0.80 kg fares at 3.0 m s⁻¹ to the right and falls upon a stationary cart B of mass 0.40 kg. The carts couple together and move off as one body. Sora takes the right as positive, and beholds the two carts as an isolated system.
greekepic.18: Substitution: (0.80 × 3.0) + (0.40 × 0) = (0.80 + 0.40) × v, so v = 2.4 ÷ 1.2 = 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right.
greekepic.19: Formula (classify): set Ek = ½mv² before against after.
greekepic.20: Substitution: Ek before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J; Ek after = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J.
greekepic.21: Answer: the kinetic energy falls from 3.6 J to 2.4 J, and so the collision is inelastic — in truth perfectly inelastic, for the carts cleave together. The 1.2 J difference is dissipated into the keeping of the world as heat, sound and deformation. Momentum, however, keeps its faith: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before, and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.
greekepic.22: Work is the energy borne to a body by a force acting through a displacement. For a constant force acting along the line of motion, the work done is the force multiplied by the displacement:
greekepic.23: When the force shifts with position, the work done is the area under a force–distance graph. This rule of the area is the selfsame one wielded for impulse upon a force–time graph, and for elastic energy upon a force–extension graph: in each, the area of a force-against-something graph yields the gathered effect of the force.
greekepic.24: Only the component of force along the displacement does work. A force aimed perpendicular to the displacement does zero work, for it has no component in the heading of motion. Herein lies the reason the speed in uniform circular motion holds constant: the net force points to the centre, the velocity is tangential, and the two stand at right angles, so the centripetal force bears no energy to the body. Forever it turns the body's direction, yet never once changes its speed.
greekepic.25: The energy a body holds by virtue of its motion is its kinetic energy:
greekepic.26: The net work done upon a body equals the change in its kinetic energy: a net force that does positive work quickens the body, and one that does negative work stays it. And because kinetic energy hangs upon the square of the speed, doubling the speed quadruples the kinetic energy.
greekepic.27: Key idea A force perpendicular to the displacement does zero work. The centripetal force in uniform circular motion stands forever perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work, and the speed holds constant though the velocity turns its heading.
greekepic.28: Connects Herein lies why a satellite in a circular orbit, and a car at steady speed upon a circular road, keep a constant speed: gravity and friction, each in turn, act toward the centre, perpendicular to the motion, and bear no energy. The work-as-area rule shall rise again for springs in the section to come.
greekepic.29: An ideal spring obeys Hooke's law: the force it wields is proportional to its extension or compression. Setting down the magnitudes, the applied force needed to hold the spring at extension x is:
greekepic.30: The spring constant k is the gradient of the force–extension graph: a stiffer spring bears a steeper line. The graph of applied force against extension is therefore a straight line through the origin, of gradient k.
greekepic.31: The elastic potential energy stored within a stretched or compressed spring is the work done in deforming it, which is the area under the force–extension graph. And because that graph is a triangle of base x and height kx, the area is ½ × x × kx:
greekepic.32: The stored energy hangs upon the square of the extension. Doubling the extension doubles the force, yet quadruples the stored energy, for the triangle's area swells with both its base and its height alike.
greekepic.33: Trap Mark well: Es = ½kx² is quadratic in x; it is not kx, neither is it ½kx. The factor of ½ springs from the triangular area beneath the force–extension graph, and the square means a spring stretched twice as far stores four times the energy, not twice. Do not confound the force F = kx (the height of the graph) with the energy Es = ½kx² (the area beneath it).
greekepic.34: A spring launcher bears a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego compresses it by 0.080 m, sets a 0.20 kg glider against it upon a frictionless horizontal track, and looses it. All the stored elastic energy is borne to the glider as kinetic energy.
greekepic.35: Formula: Es = ½kx², then energy conservation Es = Ek = ½mv².
greekepic.36: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J. Then ½ × 0.20 × v² = 0.80, so v² = 8.0.
greekepic.37: Answer: v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹, directed along the track in the heading the spring drives.
greekepic.38: Energy is neither born nor slain; it is transfigured from one form into another. The three mechanical forms in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy, the energy stored by raising a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
greekepic.39: This expression keeps its faith only within a uniform field — near the surface of the Earth, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ stands effectively constant across the height changes at hand. For the far greater height changes of a satellite, g wavers, and Eg must instead be sought from the area under a force–distance graph, or from the area under a field–distance graph multiplied by the mass (treated in the Fields chapter).
greekepic.40: In an isolated system, with no dissipative forces at work, the total mechanical energy — the sum of the kinetic, elastic and gravitational potential energies — stands unchanging. A falling ball turns gravitational potential energy into kinetic energy; a launched spring turns elastic potential energy first into kinetic energy, and then, as the body climbs, into gravitational potential energy. Following the total is oftentimes swifter than wielding forces and kinematics, for the path between the start and the end states bears no weight at all.
greekepic.41: A mass hung upon a spring and set to swinging is a ceaseless exchange of energy. At the lowest point the spring is most stretched, so elastic potential energy is greatest and kinetic energy is zero; as the mass climbs the spring relaxes and the mass quickens, so the elastic and gravitational stores turn into kinetic energy; at the highest point the kinetic energy is once more zero. In an ideal system the total holds constant and the swinging endures; in a real system a little energy is dissipated each cycle, and the swinging gradually dies away.
greekepic.42: Real systems are never perfectly isolated. Friction, drag, and the deformation of colliding bodies dissipate mechanical energy into the keeping of the world as heat, sound and lasting deformation. When dissipation befalls, the mechanical energy wanes, yet the total energy — now reckoning the heat and sound brought forth — is conserved still. The energy accounting closes exactly only when these dissipated forms are counted among the rest.
greekepic.43: Key idea In an isolated system with no dissipation, total mechanical energy (kinetic + elastic + gravitational) is conserved. When friction, drag or deformation act, mechanical energy is dissipated into the keeping of the world as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is conserved still, once those forms are counted.
greekepic.44: The launcher of the example before is now held upright. The selfsame spring (k = 250 N m⁻¹) is compressed by 0.080 m and a 0.20 kg ball is set upon its top. The ball is loosed and rises freely. Priya seeks the greatest height the ball attains above its point of release, neglecting air resistance, and takes g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
greekepic.45: Formula: with no dissipation, the elastic potential energy stored becomes gravitational potential energy at the highest point, where the ball hangs momentarily at rest: Es = mgΔh.
greekepic.46: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J, so 0.80 = 0.20 × 9.8 × Δh.
greekepic.47: Answer: Δh = 0.80 ÷ (0.20 × 9.8) = 0.41 m above the point of release. Air resistance, which dissipates energy as heat and sound, would diminish this height.
greekepic.48: Connects The selfsame bookkeeping of energy governs a projectile: a ball cast upward trades kinetic energy for gravitational potential energy on the climb, and back again on the fall, attaining the same speed at any given height (where air resistance is absent). Throughout the flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is g downward even while the ball yet rises. Air resistance dissipates energy, and lowers both the greatest height and the range.

cnpop.0: 力会改变运动，这谁都知道，但真正的王炸是另外两个守恒量——动量和能量。有了它俩，你根本不用盯着每一瞬间的力到底多大，就能把一整场碰撞或者一次发射从头算到尾。这一页我们把冲量当成力–时间图里那块面积来看，靠追踪动能把碰撞分成弹性和非弹性两类，再一路盯着能量怎么在动能、弹性势能、引力势能之间反复横跳，最后怎么悄悄耗散到环境里去。
cnpop.1: 一个物体的动量，就是它的质量乘上它的速度，p = mv。注意了，速度是矢量，所以动量也跟着是矢量——方向跟速度完全一致。在一维里处理起来很简单：钦定一个正方向，反方向的运动就当成负的来算。
cnpop.2: 合力一出手，物体的动量就会变。牛顿第二定律最一般的形式是这么说的：合力等于动量的变化率。所以在一段时间 Δt 里，合力会制造出一份动量的变化，这玩意儿就叫冲量：
cnpop.3: 冲量和动量变化，本质上是同一个东西的两种量法，所以 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹，板上钉钉。速度变化就是末速度减初速度，Δv = v − u，正负号一定得盯死：一个球弹回来，方向反了，速度就变号了，所以这时候的冲量，可以比单纯把它停下来所需的冲量还要大。
cnpop.4: 如果力在这段时间里一直变，那 FΔt 里的 F 指的就是平均力。这时候冲量可以直接从力–时间图上读出来，就是图线和时间轴之间围出来的那块面积。不管图长啥样这招都好使：恒力是个矩形，线性上升又下降的力是个三角形，先爬升、再稳住、最后落下的力就是个梯形。
cnpop.5: Trap 划重点别踩坑：动量是矢量。一辆 2.0 kg 的小车以 3.0 m s⁻¹ 运动，结果以 3.0 m s⁻¹ 弹了回来——别以为它动量变化是零，差远了。把原来的方向取成正的，动量就从 +6.0 变成了 −6.0 kg m s⁻¹，所以 Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹。永远记得给相反方向钉上 + 和 −，而且整道题里从头到尾保持一致。
cnpop.6: 小美在一条无摩擦轨道上推一辆静止的 1.5 kg 小车。一个力传感器把她施加的力记成了图 4.1 里那个梯形脉冲：力在头 0.10 s 里从 0 线性飙到 40 N，一直撑到 0.30 s 都稳在 40 N，然后在 0.40 s 线性掉回 0。推之前小车是静止的。
cnpop.7: 公式：FΔt = mΔv，其中冲量 FΔt 等于力–时间图下方的面积。
cnpop.9: 答案：冲量是 12 N s，所以 Δv = FΔt / m = 12 ÷ 1.5 = 8.0 m s⁻¹。小车一开始是静止的，所以它的末速度就是 8.0 m s⁻¹，方向跟推的方向一致。
cnpop.10: 所谓孤立系统，就是没有合外力作用在上面的系统。孤立系统的总动量是恒定的：一个物体动量涨多少，另一个就得掉多少，分毫不差——因为物体之间互相施加的力，是一对牛顿第三定律的力，作用的时间还一模一样。这就是动量守恒定律，它在每一场碰撞和爆炸里都成立，不管物体是变形、粘住、弹开还是直接碎成渣。
cnpop.11: 在一维里，选定一个正方向之后，动量守恒就写成：
cnpop.12: 动能可就是另一回事了。动能只在弹性碰撞里守恒。在一场完美的弹性碰撞里，碰后的总动能等于碰前的总动能。而在非弹性碰撞里，碰后的总动能比碰前少——但这点差额没被销毁，而是变成了别的形式：热、声音，还有永久变形所储存的能量。如果碰撞中物体粘在一起，那就是完全非弹性碰撞，在动量守恒的前提下损失掉尽可能多的动能。
cnpop.13: 想给一场碰撞分类，套路是这样的：先用动量守恒把未知的速度都解出来，再算出碰前碰后的总动能 Ek = ½mv²。两个总和相等，碰撞就是弹性的；碰后的总和更小，那就是非弹性的，少掉的那块就是耗散到环境里的能量。
cnpop.14: Key idea 在孤立系统的每一场碰撞里，动量都守恒。但动能只在弹性碰撞里守恒。如果碰后的 Ek 比碰前小，碰撞就是非弹性的，而那块消失的能量已经变成了热、声音和变形。
cnpop.15: Trap 划重点：“能量损失了”从来不代表能量凭空消失。总能量永远守恒。在非弹性碰撞里，减少的是动能；那部分能量被分毫不差地记在了账上——化成了热、声音，还有把碰撞物体永久压变形所做的功。
cnpop.16: 在一条无摩擦的气垫导轨上，质量 0.80 kg 的小车 A 以 3.0 m s⁻¹ 向右运动，撞上了静止的 0.40 kg 小车 B。两车扣在一起，连成一体一块儿跑了。小空把向右取成正方向，并把这两辆车当成一个孤立系统。
cnpop.17: 公式（碰后速度）：mAuA + mBuB = (mA + mB)v。
cnpop.19: 公式（分类）：比较碰前和碰后的 Ek = ½mv²。
cnpop.20: 代入：碰前 Ek = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J；碰后 Ek = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J。
cnpop.21: 答案：动能从 3.6 J 掉到了 2.4 J，所以这是非弹性碰撞（其实是完全非弹性，因为两车粘住了）。那 1.2 J 的差额耗散到了环境里，化成热、声音和变形。但动量呢？稳稳守恒：碰前 2.4 kg m s⁻¹，碰后 (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹。
cnpop.22: 功，就是一个力通过一段位移传递给物体的能量。对于一个沿运动方向作用的恒力，做的功就是力乘上位移：
cnpop.23: 当力随位置变化时，做的功就是力–距离图下方的面积。这条面积法则，跟力–时间图上求冲量、力–伸长图上求弹性能用的，是同一招：每一种情况下，“力对某个量”这张图的面积，给出的都是这个力累积起来的总效果。
cnpop.24: 只有沿着位移方向的力的分量才做功。一个跟位移垂直的力做的功是零，因为它在运动方向上压根没有分量。这正是匀速圆周运动里速率保持不变的原因：合力指向圆心，速度是切向的，俩货成直角，所以向心力一点能量都没传给物体。它只是不停地改变物体的方向，却从不改变它的速率。
cnpop.25: 一个物体凭借它的运动所拥有的能量，就是它的动能：
cnpop.26: 对物体做的合功，等于它动能的变化：做正功的合力让物体提速，做负功的合力让它减速。又因为动能取决于速率的平方，所以速率翻一倍，动能直接翻四倍。
cnpop.27: Key idea 跟位移垂直的力做功为零。匀速圆周运动里的向心力永远垂直于速度，所以它不做功，速率纹丝不动，哪怕速度方向一直在变。
cnpop.28: Connects 这就是为什么圆轨道上的卫星，还有在环形路上匀速跑的汽车，速率都能保持不变：引力和摩擦力分别指向圆心，垂直于运动方向，啥能量都没传。这条“功即面积”的法则，下一节讲弹簧的时候还会再登场。
cnpop.29: 一个理想弹簧遵守胡克定律：它施加的力跟它的伸长或压缩成正比。只看大小的话，把弹簧撑在伸长量 x 处所需要的施加力是：
cnpop.30: 弹簧常数 k 就是力–伸长图的斜率：弹簧越硬，线越陡。所以施加力对伸长量的图线，就是一条过原点的直线，斜率为 k。
cnpop.31: 一个被拉伸或压缩的弹簧里储存的弹性势能，就是让它变形所做的功，也就是力–伸长图下方的面积。因为这张图是个底为 x、高为 kx 的三角形，面积就是 ½ × x × kx：
cnpop.32: 储存的能量取决于伸长量的平方。伸长量翻一倍，力只翻一倍，但储存的能量翻四倍——因为三角形的面积是底和高一块儿涨的。
cnpop.33: Trap 划重点别踩坑：Es = ½kx² 是关于 x 的二次式；它不是 kx，也不是 ½kx。那个 ½ 是从力–伸长图下方的三角形面积来的，而平方意味着弹簧拉两倍远，存的能量是四倍，不是两倍。千万别把力 F = kx（图线的高）和能量 Es = ½kx²（图线下的面积）搞混了。
cnpop.34: 一个弹簧发射器的弹簧常数是 250 N m⁻¹。迪戈把它压缩了 0.080 m，在一条无摩擦的水平轨道上把一个 0.20 kg 的滑翔块顶在它上面，然后松手。所有储存的弹性能都转移给了滑翔块，变成它的动能。
cnpop.35: 公式：Es = ½kx²，然后用能量守恒 Es = Ek = ½mv²。
cnpop.36: 代入：Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J。再来 ½ × 0.20 × v² = 0.80，所以 v² = 8.0。
cnpop.37: 答案：v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹，方向沿着轨道、朝弹簧推出去的方向。
cnpop.38: 能量从不会被创造也不会被销毁，它只是从一种形式变成另一种。这门课里的三种机械能形式分别是：动能 Ek = ½mv²、弹性势能 Es = ½kx²，还有引力势能——就是把一个质量在引力场里抬高一段高度所储存的能量：
cnpop.39: 这个表达式只在匀强场里才成立——也就是地表附近，那里 g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ 在所涉及的高度变化范围内基本可以当成常数。可一旦换成卫星那种大得多的高度变化，g 就会变，这时候 Eg 就得改用力–距离图下方的面积来求，或者用场–距离图下方的面积再乘以质量（这部分在场那一章里讲）。
cnpop.40: 在一个没有耗散力的孤立系统里，总机械能——也就是动能、弹性势能和引力势能加起来的总和——是恒定的。一个下落的球把引力势能转成动能；一个发射出去的弹簧先把弹性势能转成动能，然后物体上升时再转成引力势能。盯着这个总和走，往往比硬套力和运动学要快得多，因为起点和终点之间走的是哪条路，根本无所谓。
cnpop.41: 一个挂在弹簧上、被弄得来回振荡的质量，就是一场不间断的能量交换大戏。在最低点，弹簧被拉得最长，所以弹性势能最大、动能为零；当质量往上走，弹簧放松、质量提速，于是弹性储能和引力储能一块儿转成动能；到了最高点，动能又一次归零。在理想系统里，总和保持恒定，振荡永不停歇；可在真实系统里，每一个周期都会耗散掉一丢丢能量，振荡就慢慢消停下去了。
cnpop.42: 真实系统可不是完美孤立的。摩擦、阻力，还有碰撞物体的变形，会把机械能耗散到环境里，化成热、声音和永久变形。一旦发生耗散，机械能就减少了，但总能量——现在把产生的热和声音也算进来——依然守恒。只有把这些耗散掉的形式都算进来，能量的账才能严丝合缝地对上。
cnpop.43: Key idea 在一个没有耗散的孤立系统里，总机械能（动能 + 弹性势能 + 引力势能）守恒。一旦摩擦、阻力或变形掺一脚，机械能就被耗散到环境里，化成热、声音和变形——但只要把那些形式都数进来，总能量照样守恒。
cnpop.44: 前一个例子里的那个发射器，现在被竖着拿了起来。同一根弹簧（k = 250 N m⁻¹）被压缩了 0.080 m，一个 0.20 kg 的球被放在了顶上。球被松开后自由上升。普里娅要算这个球在它的释放点上方能到达的最大高度，忽略空气阻力，并取 g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹。
cnpop.45: 公式：在没有耗散的情况下，储存的弹性势能会在最高点变成引力势能——那一刻球瞬间静止：Es = mgΔh。
cnpop.46: 代入：Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J，所以 0.80 = 0.20 × 9.8 × Δh。
cnpop.47: 答案：Δh = 0.80 ÷ (0.20 × 9.8) = 0.41 m，在释放点上方。空气阻力会把能量耗散成热和声音，所以它会让这个高度变矮一些。
cnpop.48: Connects 同一套能量记账法也管着抛体运动：一个向上抛出的球，上升时拿动能去换引力势能，下降时再换回来，在任何给定高度都能回到同样的速率（没有空气阻力的前提下）。整段飞行里，唯一的力就是引力，所以哪怕球还在往上冲，加速度也始终是 g、方向朝下。空气阻力会耗散能量，把最大高度和射程都拉低。

ai.0: Forces are forever shoving motion around — but here’s a gift: two quantities refuse to change, momentum and energy, and that lets you analyse an entire collision or launch without ever needing to know the force at every single instant. On this page you’ll read impulse straight off the area under a force–time graph, sort collisions into elastic or inelastic by following where the kinetic energy goes, and watch energy shape-shift among its kinetic, elastic and gravitational forms before leaking away into the environment.
ai.1: The momentum of an object is simply its mass times its velocity, p = mv. And because velocity is a vector, momentum inherits that vectorhood — it points the same way the velocity does. In one dimension you tame this by picking a positive direction and counting any motion the other way as negative.
ai.2: Apply a net force and you change an object’s momentum. Newton’s second law, in its fuller and more honest form, says the net force equals the rate of change of momentum — so across a time interval Δt the net force delivers a change in momentum we call the impulse:
ai.3: Impulse and change in momentum are really the same thing wearing two different name tags, which is why 1 N s = 1 kg m s⁻¹. The change in velocity is the final velocity minus the initial, Δv = v − u, and you must keep an eye on the signs: a ball that bounces back reverses direction, so its velocity flips sign and the impulse can end up larger than the impulse needed just to bring it to a halt.
ai.4: When the force keeps changing through the interval, the F sitting in FΔt is the average force. You can read the impulse off directly as the area between the graph and the time axis on a force–time graph. And it works no matter the shape: a tidy rectangle for a constant force, a triangle for a force that climbs and falls in straight lines, or a trapezium for one that ramps up, holds steady, then ramps back down.
ai.5: Trap Momentum is a vector — never forget it. A 2.0 kg trolley rolling at 3.0 m s⁻¹ that rebounds at 3.0 m s⁻¹ has not suffered zero change in momentum. Take the original direction as positive and the momentum swings from +6.0 to −6.0 kg m s⁻¹, so Δp = −6.0 − (+6.0) = −12 kg m s⁻¹. Always hand out + and − to opposite directions, and stay consistent with them all the way through a problem.
ai.6: Mei gives a stationary 1.5 kg trolley a shove along a frictionless track. A force sensor captures the force she applies as the trapezoidal pulse you see in Figure 4.1: the force climbs in a straight line from 0 to 40 N over the first 0.10 s, holds steady at 40 N until 0.30 s, then drops linearly back to 0 at 0.40 s. The trolley is at rest before she pushes.
ai.7: Formula: FΔt = mΔv, where the impulse FΔt is the area under the force–time graph.
ai.9: Answer: the impulse comes to 12 N s, so Δv = FΔt / m = 12 ÷ 1.5 = 8.0 m s⁻¹. The trolley set off from rest, so its final velocity is 8.0 m s⁻¹ in the direction of the push.
ai.10: An isolated system is one with no net external force acting on it. The total momentum of such a system never budges: whatever momentum one object gains, another gives up by exactly the same amount — because the forces the objects exert on each other form a Newton’s third law pair, equal and opposite, acting for the same length of time. This is the law of conservation of momentum, and it holds in every collision and explosion you can dream up, whether the objects deform, stick, bounce or shatter.
ai.11: In one dimension, once you’ve nailed down a positive direction, conservation of momentum is written:
ai.12: Kinetic energy, though, is a different beast. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. In a perfectly elastic collision the total kinetic energy afterwards exactly matches the total before. In an inelastic collision the total kinetic energy afterwards comes up short; that missing energy isn’t destroyed — it’s transformed into other forms: heat, sound and the energy locked into permanent deformation. A collision where the objects clump together is perfectly inelastic, and it sheds the greatest possible kinetic energy that conserving momentum will still allow.
ai.13: To classify a collision, you lean on momentum conservation first to pin down any unknown velocities, then you compute the total kinetic energy Ek = ½mv² both before and after. If the two totals match, the collision is elastic; if the total after has shrunk, it’s inelastic, and the shortfall is precisely the energy dissipated to the environment.
ai.14: Key idea Momentum is conserved in every collision in an isolated system. Kinetic energy is conserved only in an elastic collision. If Ek after is less than Ek before, the collision is inelastic and the missing energy has turned into heat, sound and deformation.
ai.15: Trap “Energy is lost” never means energy vanishes. Total energy is always conserved — full stop. In an inelastic collision it is the kinetic energy that drops; that energy is accounted for to the last joule as heat, sound and the work spent permanently deforming the colliding objects.
ai.16: On a frictionless air track, cart A of mass 0.80 kg glides at 3.0 m s⁻¹ to the right and slams into a stationary cart B of mass 0.40 kg. The carts couple together and move off as a single unit. Sora takes the right as positive and treats the two carts as an isolated system.
ai.18: Substitution: (0.80 × 3.0) + (0.40 × 0) = (0.80 + 0.40) × v, so v = 2.4 ÷ 1.2 = 2.0 m s⁻¹ to the right.
ai.19: Formula (classify): compare Ek = ½mv² before and after.
ai.20: Substitution: Ek before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² = 3.6 J; Ek after = ½ × 1.2 × 2.0² = 2.4 J.
ai.21: Answer: the kinetic energy slides from 3.6 J down to 2.4 J, so the collision is inelastic (perfectly inelastic, in fact, because the carts stick fast). The 1.2 J difference is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation. Momentum, on the other hand, holds firm: 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ before and (1.2 × 2.0) = 2.4 kg m s⁻¹ after.
ai.22: Work is the energy a force hands to an object as it acts through a displacement. For a constant force acting along the line of motion, the work done is just the force multiplied by the displacement:
ai.23: When the force changes with position, the work done becomes the area under a force–distance graph. It’s the very same area trick you used for impulse on a force–time graph, and the one you’ll use for elastic energy on a force–extension graph: in every case, the area of a force-versus-something graph hands you the accumulated effect of the force.
ai.24: Only the component of force along the displacement does any work. A force aimed perpendicular to the displacement does zero work, because it has no component in the direction of motion at all. This is exactly why the speed in uniform circular motion stays put: the net force points to the centre, the velocity runs tangentially, and the two sit at right angles — so the centripetal force transfers no energy to the object whatsoever. It endlessly reshapes the object’s direction without ever touching its speed.
ai.25: The energy an object owns purely by virtue of moving is its kinetic energy:
ai.26: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy: a net force that does positive work speeds the object up, and one that does negative work slows it down. And because kinetic energy hangs on the square of the speed, doubling the speed quadruples the kinetic energy.
ai.27: Key idea A force perpendicular to the displacement does zero work. The centripetal force in uniform circular motion is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work and the speed stays constant even as the velocity keeps changing direction.
ai.28: Connects This is why a satellite in a circular orbit, and a car cruising at steady speed on a circular road, keep a constant speed: gravity and friction respectively pull toward the centre, perpendicular to the motion, and so transfer no energy. And the work-as-area rule will be back — it returns for springs in the next section.
ai.29: An ideal spring obeys Hooke’s law: the force it pushes back with is proportional to how far it’s stretched or squashed. Writing the magnitudes, the applied force needed to hold the spring at extension x is:
ai.30: The spring constant k is the gradient of the force–extension graph: a stiffer spring gives a steeper line. So the graph of applied force against extension is a straight line through the origin, with gradient k.
ai.31: The elastic potential energy stashed in a stretched or compressed spring is the work done in deforming it — which is the area under the force–extension graph. Since that graph is a triangle with base x and height kx, the area is ½ × x × kx:
ai.32: The stored energy depends on the square of the extension. Double the extension and you double the force but quadruple the stored energy — because the triangle’s area swells with both its base and its height at once.
ai.33: Trap Es = ½kx² is quadratic in x; it is not kx and it is not ½kx. The factor of ½ comes straight from the triangular area under the force–extension graph, and the square means a spring stretched twice as far stores four times the energy, not twice. Don’t muddle the force F = kx (the height of the graph) with the energy Es = ½kx² (the area beneath it).
ai.34: A spring launcher has a spring constant of 250 N m⁻¹. Diego compresses it by 0.080 m, sets a 0.20 kg glider against it on a frictionless horizontal track, and lets it go. All the stored elastic energy passes into the glider as kinetic energy.
ai.35: Formula: Es = ½kx², then energy conservation Es = Ek = ½mv².
ai.36: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J. Then ½ × 0.20 × v² = 0.80, so v² = 8.0.
ai.37: Answer: v = √8.0 = 2.8 m s⁻¹, directed along the track the way the spring pushes.
ai.38: Energy is never made and never destroyed; it just changes its outfit, transforming from one form into another. The three mechanical forms in this study are kinetic energy Ek = ½mv², elastic potential energy Es = ½kx², and gravitational potential energy — the energy you bank by raising a mass through a height in a gravitational field:
ai.39: This expression only holds in a uniform field — near Earth’s surface, where g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹ stays effectively constant over the height changes involved. For the far bigger height changes of a satellite, g itself varies, and Eg has to be found instead from the area under a force–distance graph, or from the area under a field–distance graph multiplied by the mass (you’ll meet this in the Fields chapter).
ai.40: In an isolated system with no dissipative forces, the total mechanical energy — the sum of the kinetic, elastic and gravitational potential energies — stays constant. A falling ball pours gravitational potential energy into kinetic energy; a launched spring tips elastic potential energy first into kinetic energy and then, as the object climbs, into gravitational potential energy. Following the total is often quicker than wrestling with forces and kinematics, because the path between the start and end states simply doesn’t matter.
ai.41: A mass dangling on a spring and set swinging is one long, continuous energy exchange. At the lowest point the spring is stretched the most, so elastic potential energy peaks and kinetic energy is zero; as the mass rises the spring relaxes and the mass picks up speed, so the elastic and gravitational stores pour into kinetic energy; at the highest point the kinetic energy drops to zero again. In an ideal system the total holds constant and the oscillation runs on forever; in a real system a little energy bleeds away each cycle and the oscillation gradually fades to nothing.
ai.42: Real systems are never perfectly isolated. Friction, drag and the deformation of colliding bodies dissipate mechanical energy into the environment as heat, sound and permanent deformation. When dissipation kicks in the mechanical energy drops, but the total energy — now counting the heat and sound produced — is still conserved. The energy accounting only balances exactly once you let those dissipated forms back into the books.
ai.43: Key idea In an isolated system with no dissipation, total mechanical energy (kinetic + elastic + gravitational) is conserved. When friction, drag or deformation act, mechanical energy is dissipated to the environment as heat, sound and deformation, and the total energy is still conserved once those forms are counted.
ai.44: The launcher from the previous example is now stood up vertically. The same spring (k = 250 N m⁻¹) is compressed by 0.080 m and a 0.20 kg ball is set on top. The ball is released and rises freely. Priya wants the maximum height the ball reaches above its release point, ignoring air resistance, and takes g = 9.8 N kg⁻¹.
ai.45: Formula: with no dissipation, the elastic potential energy stored becomes gravitational potential energy at the highest point, where the ball hangs momentarily at rest: Es = mgΔh.
ai.46: Substitution: Es = ½ × 250 × 0.080² = 0.80 J, so 0.80 = 0.20 × 9.8 × Δh.
ai.47: Answer: Δh = 0.80 ÷ (0.20 × 9.8) = 0.41 m above the release point. Air resistance, which drains energy off as heat and sound, would trim this height down.
ai.48: Connects The same energy bookkeeping rules a projectile: a ball thrown upward trades kinetic energy for gravitational potential energy on the way up and trades it straight back on the way down, hitting the same speed at any given height (with no air resistance to spoil it). For the whole flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is g downward even while the ball is still climbing. Air resistance dissipates energy and shaves down both the maximum height and the range.
